## 1. Executive Summary

The Universe Simulation Game is an extraordinarily ambitious browser-based 3D game that attempts to simulate the entire history of a universe — from the Big Bang through to the emergence of intelligent civilizations — within a single web application. The player begins as a primitive microorganism and, over the course of many simulation steps spanning billions of years of in-game time, can evolve, grow, build civilizations, research technology, and ultimately gain the power to control the physical constants of reality itself.

The project is currently in its very earliest stage of development. A substantial amount of foundational code has been written — covering physics simulation, chemical reactions, genetic systems, artificial intelligence, and world generation — but the game is not yet playable in any traditional sense. There is no main menu, no tutorial, no win condition that works, and most gameplay features described in the design documents are not yet connected to the game world. What exists is a carefully engineered technical foundation: the scaffolding upon which the game will eventually be built.

The technical ambition of this project is genuinely remarkable. The development team has implemented real scientific equations from physics, chemistry, and biology textbooks. Chemical reactions use the Arrhenius equation (the actual formula scientists use to predict reaction rates). Genetic mutation uses real mutation rate statistics from molecular biology. Ecosystem populations are modeled using the same Lotka-Volterra equations used by ecologists. The AI for creatures uses a planning algorithm originally developed for the game F.E.A.R. This is not a superficial simulation — it is an attempt to model reality from first principles.

The current state is best described as: all the instruments are on the stage, the orchestra pit is built, the seats are installed, but the musicians have not yet sat down and the audience has not yet arrived. The infrastructure is impressive; the experience is not yet there. The project appears to be the work of a technically sophisticated developer who has prioritized scientific accuracy and architectural correctness above all else.

For anyone evaluating this project: this is a long-term construction project, not a finished product. The foundation is solid. The vision is extraordinary. But a great deal of work remains before this becomes something a player can meaningfully enjoy.

---

## 2. Application Overview

### What is this game?

The Universe Simulation Game is a browser-based 3D simulation game where the player experiences the entire lifespan of a universe. The game is structured in seven phases:

1. **Physics Phase** — The universe begins. Particles form. Forces emerge.
2. **Chemistry Phase** — Atoms bond into molecules. Chemical reactions occur.
3. **Biology Phase** — Life emerges from chemistry. Creatures evolve, compete, and reproduce.
4. **AI NPC Phase** — Creatures develop intelligence. They have goals, emotions, memories, and can communicate.
5. **World Phase** — The player inhabits a planet with weather, geology, and biomes.
6. **Player Phase** — The player controls a creature, gathers resources, crafts tools, and survives.
7. **Civilization Phase** — Societies form, technology is researched, and eventually the player can rewrite the laws of physics.

### Who is it for?

This game targets players who enjoy deeply complex simulation games — people who play titles like Dwarf Fortress, Caves of Qud, or Spore, but who want something built on actual science. It is also clearly a passion project for a developer fascinated by physics, chemistry, and biology.

### What problems does it solve?

No game currently offers a truly science-accurate universe simulation that spans from quantum physics to civilizational collapse in a single coherent experience. This project attempts to fill that gap.

### Technology Stack (plain English)

| Technology | What it does |
|-----------|-------------|
| Vite | Builds and serves the application to the browser |
| React 18 | Manages the user interface panels and menus |
| TypeScript | The programming language used — adds type safety to JavaScript |
| Three.js | Renders the 3D world in the browser |
| @react-three/fiber | Connects Three.js to React so the 3D scene responds to game state |
| Rapier3D (WASM) | A physics engine compiled from Rust — handles rigid body collisions |
| bitecs | Stores all game entities (creatures, items, etc.) in highly efficient typed memory |
| Zustand | Stores global game state (paused, current time, player vitals) |
| SharedArrayBuffer | Lets multiple parallel processing threads share the same memory safely |
| Web Workers | Runs physics, fluid, thermal, and chemical simulations on background threads |
| IndexedDB (idb) | Planned save system — stores game progress in the browser's built-in database |

## 3. Project Structure

The project is organized into the following folder structure. Every folder has a clear purpose.

```
universe-sim/
├── src/                        Main source code
│   ├── ai/                     Creature intelligence systems
│   │   ├── EmotionModel.ts     16-emotion model with decay over time
│   │   ├── GOAP.ts             Goal-Oriented Action Planning (A* search)
│   │   ├── LLMBridge.ts        Connects to Claude/GPT for advanced NPC dialogue
│   │   └── NeuralArchitecture.ts  Five-tier brain system (reflex to abstract reasoning)
│   ├── biology/                Life simulation
│   │   ├── EcosystemBalance.ts Population dynamics (predator-prey equations)
│   │   ├── GenomeEncoder.ts    256-bit DNA system encoding creature traits
│   │   └── MutationEngine.ts   Six types of genetic mutation
│   ├── chemistry/              Chemical simulation
│   │   ├── ElementDatabase.ts  All 118 elements with real physical properties
│   │   └── ReactionEngine.ts   60+ chemical reactions using Arrhenius kinetics
│   ├── civilization/           Society and technology
│   │   └── TechTree.ts         150-node tech tree from Stone Age to Universe Control
│   ├── ecs/                    Entity Component System (game object management)
│   │   ├── world.ts            Component definitions and entity factories
│   │   └── systems/
│   │       └── MetabolismSystem.ts  Hunger, thirst, energy, and death logic
│   ├── engine/                 Core simulation engine
│   │   ├── Grid.ts             3D world grid using shared memory
│   │   ├── SimClock.ts         Time dilation from 0.01x to 1,000,000x real time
│   │   ├── SimulationEngine.ts Coordinates all simulation threads
│   │   ├── constants.ts        Real scientific constants (NIST CODATA 2022)
│   │   └── workers/            Background simulation threads
│   │       ├── physics.worker.ts   Gravity
│   │       ├── fluid.worker.ts     Fluid dynamics (Navier-Stokes simplified)
│   │       ├── thermal.worker.ts   Heat conduction and radiation
│   │       └── chem.worker.ts      Chemistry (placeholder — not yet active)
│   ├── rendering/              3D visual output
│   │   ├── SceneRoot.tsx       Main 3D scene setup
│   │   └── entities/
│   │       └── CreatureRenderer.tsx  Renders up to 10,000 creatures efficiently
│   ├── store/                  Global state management
│   │   ├── gameStore.ts        Game state (paused, time, epoch)
│   │   └── playerStore.ts      Player state (health, hunger, goals, evolution points)
│   ├── ui/                     User interface
│   │   ├── HUD.tsx             On-screen display (vitals, clock, time controls)
│   │   └── TimeControls.tsx    Pause and time speed buttons
│   ├── world/                  Planet and environment generation
│   │   └── PlanetGenerator.ts  Procedural planet with tectonic plates and biomes
│   ├── App.tsx                 Root application component
│   └── main.tsx                Application entry point
├── docs/
│   └── GAME_DESIGN.md          1,067-line design document (full game vision)
├── rust/                       Empty — planned Rust server code
├── server/                     Empty — planned server code
├── package.json                Dependencies and scripts
└── vite.config.ts              Build configuration
```

### Key Files at a Glance

- **`docs/GAME_DESIGN.md`** — The authoritative vision document. Everything the game is intended to become.
- **`src/engine/constants.ts`** — All physics constants are exact NIST CODATA 2022 values. This is the scientific backbone of the simulation.
- **`src/chemistry/ReactionEngine.ts`** — The most complex single file: 60+ chemical reactions all modeled with real Arrhenius kinetics.
- **`src/biology/GenomeEncoder.ts`** — 256-bit DNA system that encodes over 40 creature traits, including dominant/recessive allele logic.
- **`src/civilization/TechTree.ts`** — 150 technology nodes from stone tools to the ability to rewrite physical constants.

---

## 4. Features and Functionality

### 4.1 Physics Engine

The physics engine operates on a 3D grid of 131,072 cells (64 wide × 32 tall × 64 deep). Each cell in this grid stores 25 data values: what material it contains, its temperature, pressure, density, velocity in three directions, up to eight different chemical compounds, and the amount of energy and light present.

Four separate background threads process different aspects of physics simultaneously:

**Gravity** — Applies Earth-like gravity (9.80665 meters per second squared, exactly the value used by scientists) to all moving objects. Velocity is capped at 100 meters per second to prevent numerical instability.

**Fluid Dynamics** — Implements a simplified version of the Navier-Stokes equations (the equations that govern how fluids flow in the real world). Pressure differences push fluids from high-pressure to low-pressure regions. Viscous drag slows fluid movement over time.

**Heat Transfer** — Implements Fourier's law of heat conduction (heat flows from hot to cold, at a rate proportional to the temperature difference). Cells above 500 degrees Celsius also radiate heat via the Stefan-Boltzmann law (the same physics that governs how stars radiate energy).

**Rigid Body Physics** — Rapier3D (a physics engine compiled from Rust) handles solid object collisions — creatures bumping into walls, items falling to the ground, and so on.

The time system is particularly sophisticated. The player can speed up simulation time by factors ranging from 0.1x (slower than real time) to 1,000,000x (one million times faster than real time). At maximum speed, one second of real time corresponds to about 11.5 days of simulation time. This allows the game to span geological and cosmological timescales.

### 4.2 Chemistry Engine

The chemistry engine is one of the most scientifically rigorous components in the project. It models chemical reactions using the Arrhenius equation:

> k = A × exp(-Ea / RT)

In plain English: every chemical reaction has a rate that depends on temperature. Higher temperatures mean reactions happen faster. Each reaction has an activation energy (the energy needed to start the reaction) and a pre-exponential factor (how likely the reaction is to occur when the conditions are met).

The system contains 60+ reactions covering:

- **Combustion** — Methane burns, hydrogen explodes, ethanol ignites. Each fuel has its own real activation energy.
- **Biological chemistry** — Glycolysis (how cells break down sugar), the TCA cycle (how cells extract energy from sugar), cellular respiration (oxygen-based energy production), fermentation (anaerobic energy production).
- **Geochemistry** — Serpentinization (how water reacts with ocean-floor rock to produce hydrogen — a leading theory for the origin of life), weathering of pyrite (iron sulfide reacting with oxygen and water to form sulfuric acid).
- **Atmospheric chemistry** — Nitrogen fixation (the Haber-Bosch process), acid rain formation, Fischer-Tropsch synthesis (how complex organic molecules form from simple gases).
- **Abiogenesis reactions** — The Miller-Urey reaction (how amino acids can form from simple gases under lightning), the Strecker synthesis (another amino acid formation pathway), the formose reaction (how simple sugars can form from formaldehyde).

All 118 chemical elements are modeled with their real physical properties: melting point, boiling point, density, electronegativity, oxidation states, thermal conductivity, electrical resistance, and color.

### 4.3 Biology Engine

The biology system has three major components: genetics, mutation, and ecosystem dynamics.

**Genetics (GenomeEncoder.ts)**

Every creature in the simulation has a 256-bit genome — equivalent to 32 bytes of data, like a very compact DNA sequence. This genome encodes over 40 different traits:

| Genome Region | Bits | Traits Encoded |
|--------------|------|---------------|
| Body Plan | 0-127 | Size, mass, limbs, symmetry, metabolism rate, vision, hearing, smell, locomotion speed, defense, offense |
| Reproduction | 80-95 | Reproduction rate, gestation period, clutch size, parental care |
| Neural | 96-127 | Brain complexity level (0-4), memory capacity, learning rate |
| Social | 128-159 | Group size preference, hierarchy tendency, altruism, aggression |
| Communication | 160-191 | Language complexity, signal range, signal types |
| Development | 192-223 | Lifespan, growth rate, developmental stages |
| Civilization | 224-255 | Tool use aptitude, curiosity, cooperation instinct |

The genome uses dominant/recessive allele logic (like real genetics). Each trait is encoded at two genomic positions; one position "wins" if it encodes the dominant variant. This means genetic crosses between parents produce offspring with realistic trait mixing.

**Mutation (MutationEngine.ts)**

Mutations occur at a base rate of 1 in 1 billion bits per generation — the same order of magnitude as real biological mutation rates. Six types of mutation are modeled:

1. **Point mutation** — A single bit flips (most common, 1x rate)
2. **Insertion** — New genetic material is inserted (0.1x rate)
3. **Deletion** — Genetic material is removed (0.1x rate)
4. **Transposition** — A section of genome jumps to a new location (0.01x rate)
5. **Inversion** — A section of genome is reversed (0.005x rate)
6. **Duplication** — A section of genome is copied and appended (0.005x rate)
7. **Horizontal gene transfer** — Simulating viral transduction and bacterial conjugation — genes can transfer between species (1,000x rarer than point mutations)

Environmental mutagens (like radiation) can multiply mutation rates by up to 1,000x. The system checks for lethal mutations — mutations that destroy critical metabolic genes are discarded.

**Ecosystem Dynamics (EcosystemBalance.ts)**

Populations of species are tracked using the Generalized Lotka-Volterra equations — the same mathematical model used by ecologists to predict predator-prey population cycles (the classic example is the oscillating populations of wolves and rabbits in Yellowstone). The simulation uses RK4 integration (a high-accuracy numerical method) to solve these equations.

Real carrying capacity values are used for 15 biomes and 6 trophic levels (food chain levels). For example, a tropical rainforest supports far more biomass than a desert.

The system also models the Red Queen hypothesis — the evolutionary arms race between predators and prey. As one species evolves better defenses, the other evolves better attacks. The simulation tracks escalation scores and can detect when an arms race is occurring.

Populations below 50 individuals are flagged as at risk of extinction.

### 4.4 Artificial Intelligence System

The AI system is the most complex and layered part of the project. Creatures can have one of five levels of intelligence, and higher-level creatures have genuinely more sophisticated behavior.

**Level 0 — Reflex (Microbes, simple invertebrates)**
Pure stimulus-response. The creature moves toward food if it senses it, away from danger if it senses it, and performs a random walk otherwise. No memory, no planning.

**Level 1 — Instinct (Fish, insects)**
An 8-state finite state machine (a system with a fixed set of states and rules for switching between them). States include: wandering, fleeing, eating, sleeping, mating, attacking. Emotions modulate which state is chosen. A frightened creature is more likely to flee; a hungry creature is more likely to seek food.

**Level 2 — Learning (Mammals, birds)**
A behavior tree (a hierarchical structure of decisions) with Q-learning weight updates. Q-learning is a form of reinforcement learning — the creature learns from experience which behaviors lead to good outcomes. The weights are stored per creature and updated based on rewards and punishments.

**Level 3 — Planning (Great apes, early humans)**
GOAP (Goal-Oriented Action Planning) using A* search (the same pathfinding algorithm used in most strategy games, but applied to abstract planning rather than physical navigation). The creature defines a goal (survive, reproduce, build shelter) and searches for a sequence of actions that achieves that goal.

20 standard actions are available: finding food, eating, drinking, finding shelter, sleeping, finding a mate, mating, fleeing, attacking, hunting, gathering materials, crafting tools, building shelter, teaching skills, learning skills, trading, exploring, calling for help, grooming allies.

**Level 4 — Abstract Reasoning (Humans, advanced AI)**
GOAP planning combined with optional LLM (Large Language Model) override. The creature can query an actual AI model (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI GPT, or a local Ollama model) to generate dialogue and complex decisions. The LLM receives a detailed context including the creature's name, role, settlement, emotional state, recent memories, relationships, current goal, knowledge, civilization tier, and history with the player.

**Emotion Model (EmotionModel.ts)**

Creatures have 16 emotions based on the OCC (Ortony-Clore-Collins) cognitive model from emotion psychology:

joy, distress, hope, fear, satisfaction, disappointment, pride, shame, love, hate, gratitude, anger, curiosity, boredom, disgust, surprise

Each emotion has a half-life (how long it lasts before fading). Surprise fades in 2 seconds; love lasts an hour; hate lasts two hours. Emotions influence GOAP goal priorities — a frightened creature prioritizes fleeing over eating, for example.

Events appraise into emotional responses. Witnessing violence triggers fear and distress. Completing a goal triggers satisfaction. Forming a bond triggers love.

### 4.5 World Generation

The world is a procedurally generated planet — no two planets are the same.

**Terrain (PlanetGenerator.ts)**

12 tectonic plates are generated, each moving at realistic speeds (1-10 centimeters per year, the actual rate of real tectonic plates). Where plates collide, mountains form (up to +5,000 meters). Where oceanic plates meet, trenches form (down to -2,000 meters). Height is generated using Fractal Brownian Motion noise — a mathematical technique that produces natural-looking terrain with detail at multiple scales.

**Temperature and Precipitation**

Temperature is computed from:
- Latitude (the equator is hotter than the poles — modeled with a cosine function)
- Altitude (temperature drops 6.5°C per kilometer of elevation — the International Standard Atmosphere lapse rate)
- Greenhouse gas concentration (using logarithmic forcing — the same formula climate scientists use)

Precipitation is computed using the Hadley cell structure (the real atmospheric circulation pattern that creates tropical rainforests near the equator and deserts around 30° latitude) plus an orographic effect (mountains block moisture, creating wet windward sides and dry rain shadows).

**Biome Classification**

The Whittaker biome classification system (an actual scientific framework) determines biome type from temperature and precipitation. This produces realistic biomes: tundra, boreal forest, temperate rainforest, tropical rainforest, savanna, grassland, desert, shrubland, and so on.

**Planet Properties**

Each planet has: seed (for reproducibility), radius, mass, axial tilt, orbital period, rotation period, atmospheric composition, surface temperature, and water fraction. Surface gravity is computed from the real formula (G × mass / radius²).

Chunk size is 64×64 cells at 4 meters per cell — each chunk covers a 256×256 meter area.

### 4.6 Player Gameplay

The player controls a single creature entity. The player's experience is intended to span from microorganism to civilization builder.

**Player Vitals**

The player has five vital statistics tracked in real time:

| Vital | Starting Value | Mechanics |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| Health | 100% | Damaged by starvation (2 HP/s at >95% hunger) and dehydration (5 HP/s at >98% thirst). Regenerates slowly when fed. |
| Satiety (Hunger) | 0% hungry | Full starvation takes approximately 9.25 hours of real time (at 1x speed). |
| Hydration (Thirst) | 0% thirsty | Full dehydration takes approximately 5.56 hours of real time (at 1x speed). |
| Energy | 100% | Depleted by activity. Regenerates during rest. |
| Stamina | 100% | Fatigue accumulates over time. Maximum fatigue reached in approximately 27.8 hours. |

**Evolution Points**

The player earns Evolution Points (EP) by making discoveries. These points are used to unlock traits, research technology, and advance through civilization tiers.

**Current Goal System**

The player always has a current goal displayed in the HUD. Goals are plain-language strings like "survive," "find food," or "build shelter." The goal system tracks discoveries the player has made.

**Intended Gameplay Loop (Designed, Not Yet Implemented)**

The design document describes a rich gameplay loop:
- Start as a microorganism. Hunt nutrients. Survive long enough to reproduce.
- Evolve into a multicellular organism, then an animal, then a social creature.
- Form a tribe. Gather resources. Craft tools. Build structures.
- Research technology through 150 nodes, from stone tools to interstellar travel.
- Ultimately unlock the ability to modify the physical constants of the simulation itself.

### 4.7 Civilization System

**Technology Tree (TechTree.ts)**

The technology tree contains 150 nodes organized into 10 civilization tiers:

| Tier | Name | Example Technologies |
|------|------|---------------------|
| 0 | Stone Age | Fire Making, Flint Knapping, Shelter Building |
| 1 | Neolithic | Agriculture, Animal Domestication, Pottery |
| 2 | Bronze Age | Smelting, Writing, Wheel, Sailing |
| 3 | Iron Age | Iron Working, Mathematics, Aqueducts |
| 4 | Medieval | Steel Making, Windmills, Universities |
| 5 | Industrial | Steam Engine, Electricity, Germ Theory |
| 6 | Modern | Computers, Nuclear Power, Space Exploration |
| 7 | Information Age | Artificial Intelligence, Genetic Engineering |
| 8 | Transhuman | Brain Uploading, Nanotechnology, Dyson Sphere |
| 9 | Simulation Age | Universal Emulation, Physical Constants Control |

Each technology node has:
- Prerequisites (which technologies must be researched first)
- An Evolution Points cost
- A research time
- A description
- A list of what it unlocks
- A historical analog (which real-world technology it corresponds to)

The final two nodes — "Universe Simulation Technology" and "Physical Constants Control" — represent the ultimate goal: the player has become so advanced they can rewrite the laws of physics governing the simulation they live in.

---

## 5. What the Player Can Do and Experience

### What is Currently Playable

At this stage of development, a player who launches the application will see:

1. A 3D scene with a flat green plane, a sky, and stars.
2. A heads-up display (HUD) in the corner showing vital statistics (health, satiety, hydration, energy, stamina).
3. A time display in the top-right showing the current simulation time and cosmological epoch.
4. Time control buttons at the bottom of the screen — pause, and speed multipliers from 0.1x to 1,000,000x.

The player cannot yet:
- Move a character
- Interact with any objects
- See creatures in the world (the renderer exists but no creatures are spawned)
- Make meaningful decisions
- Progress toward any goal

The vitals in the HUD do not yet change in real time (the metabolism system exists in code but is not connected to a running game loop). The time controls work — the player can pause and adjust speed, and the epoch display updates correctly.

### What the Player Will Eventually Experience (Designed Intent)

The game is designed to deliver the following experience arc:

**Early Game — The Primordial World**
The player wakes as a microscopic organism in a warm chemical soup. They perceive the world through chemical gradients and light. They must hunt nutrient molecules to survive, divide to reproduce, and avoid being consumed by competing organisms. Evolution happens over generations — the player's offspring inherit and slightly vary the parent's genome.

**Mid Game — The Age of Animals**
Having evolved into a multicellular organism and eventually a complex animal, the player navigates a world rich with other creatures. Predators must be avoided. Food must be found and consumed. Mates must be courted. Eventually the player's creature develops enough neural complexity to use tools, build structures, and communicate with other intelligent beings.

**Late Game — Civilization**
The player guides a growing civilization through the 150-node technology tree. Resources must be gathered, buildings constructed, alliances formed, wars fought or avoided. NPCs have real emotions, goals, and opinions — the player can form relationships with them and even have conversations powered by actual AI language models.

**End Game — Universe Control**
Having researched all technology up to Tier 9, the player gains the ability to modify the physical constants of the simulation — changing gravity, the speed of light, the strength of electromagnetism. The final revelation: the player's universe is itself a simulation, and they have become the simulator.

---

## 6. User Interface and Design

### Current Interface

The interface currently consists of three panels:

**Top-Left: Vitals Panel**

A semi-transparent dark panel (glass morphism style) displaying five progress bars:
- HEALTH (red)
- SATIETY (orange) — shown as 1 - hunger, so full satiety = full bar
- HYDRATION (blue) — shown as 1 - thirst
- ENERGY (green)
- STAMINA (purple) — shown as 1 - fatigue

Each bar animates smoothly (0.3 second transitions). Bars below 25% flash red to indicate danger. Below the bars is the Evolution Points counter (EP) in yellow, and a current goal badge in blue.

**Top-Right: Simulation Clock**

Displays:
- The current cosmological epoch (e.g., "STELLAR", "DARK AGES", "REIONIZATION") in small grey text
- The formatted simulation time (e.g., "0.0 s", "4.32 Gyr") in large bold white text
- A "PAUSED" indicator in red when the simulation is paused

The epoch names are drawn from real Big Bang cosmology: Planck epoch, Grand Unification, Electroweak, Quark Epoch, Nucleosynthesis, Photon Epoch, Dark Ages, Reionization, Stellar, Galactic, Contemporary, Stellar Late, Degenerate, Dark Era.

**Bottom-Center: Time Controls**

A row of buttons: a pause/resume toggle, and nine time speed buttons (0.1×, 0.5×, 1×, 10×, 100×, 1k×, 10k×, 100k×, 1M×). The active speed is highlighted in yellow. The pause button is green when running and red when paused.

### Visual Design

The interface uses a consistent dark, minimalist style with:
- Semi-transparent black backgrounds with blur (glassmorphism)
- Monospace fonts throughout
- A color vocabulary: red = danger/health, orange = hunger/warmth, blue = thirst/hydration, green = energy/alive, purple = fatigue, yellow = evolution points/active selection

The 3D scene uses:
- A flat green plane (512m × 512m) representing terrain
- A procedurally generated sky
- 5,000 stars
- Directional sunlight with shadow mapping (2048×2048 shadow resolution)

### Navigation

There is no navigation yet — no menus, no screens to switch between. The entire application is a single 3D view with a HUD overlay.

## 7. Current Implementation State

This section gives an honest assessment of what is done versus what is planned.

### Implementation Milestone Status

The project defines 21 milestones (M0 through M20). Current status:

| Milestone | Description | Status |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| M0 | Project scaffolding, ECS foundation, rendering pipeline, basic HUD | Complete |
| M1 | Grid physics, thermal, fluid, rigid body | Skeleton implemented |
| M2 | Full chemistry with reactions | Skeleton implemented |
| M3 | Genome/evolution, ecosystem balance | Skeleton implemented |
| M4 | Neural AI levels 0-2, emotion model | Skeleton implemented |
| M5 | Planet generator, biomes, weather | Skeleton implemented |
| M6 | Player controls, survival mechanics | Not yet connected |
| M7 | GOAP planning (L3 AI), basic NPC dialogue | Skeleton implemented |
| M8 | LLM NPC bridge | Skeleton implemented |
| M9 | Inventory, crafting, building | Not yet started |
| M10 | Civilization system, tech tree research | Skeleton implemented |
| M11 | Abiogenesis automatic event | Not yet started |
| M12 | Multiplayer/server | Not yet started |
| M13-M20 | Advanced features, balancing, release | Not yet started |

| Player movement | No | No | No |
| Inventory/crafting | No | No | No |
| Building system | No | No | No |
| Abiogenesis event | No | No | No |
| Discovery journal | No | No | No |
| Evolution tree UI | No | No | No |
| Main menu | No | No | No |
| Tutorial | No | No | No |

### Missing Files (Referenced in Design but Not Yet Created)

The following files are mentioned in the game design document but do not yet exist in the codebase:

- `src/chemistry/MoleculeRegistry.ts` — Registry of molecular compounds
- `src/biology/SpeciesRegistry.ts` — Track all species that have evolved
- `src/biology/Abiogenesis.ts` — The origin of life event
- `src/world/WorldChunkManager.ts` — Chunk loading and streaming
- `src/world/WeatherSystem.ts` — Dynamic weather simulation
- `src/world/BiomeRegistry.ts` — Biome data and transitions
- `src/player/PlayerController.ts` — Player input and movement
- `src/player/Inventory.ts` — Item storage and management
- `src/player/BuildingSystem.ts` — Construction mechanics
- `src/ui/EvolutionTree.tsx` — Visual trait progression
- `src/ui/DiscoveryJournal.tsx` — Discovered knowledge log
- `src/ui/CivilizationTracker.tsx` — Civilization overview panel
- `src/systems/SaveSystem.ts` — Game save and load functionality
- `src/rendering/entities/ProceduralCreature.tsx` — Creature visual models

---

## 8. Technical Overview in Plain Language

### How the Game Engine Works

Imagine the game world as a giant three-dimensional spreadsheet. Each cell in the spreadsheet (and there are 131,072 of them) stores information: what material is here, how hot it is, how much pressure there is, how fast things are moving, and what chemicals are present.

Every 16.67 milliseconds (60 times per second), the game updates this entire spreadsheet. But updating 131,072 cells 60 times per second would be too slow if done one at a time, so the work is divided among four background threads (like having four workers each responsible for different types of calculations):

- Worker 1: Apply gravity to all cells
- Worker 2: Simulate fluid flow between cells
- Worker 3: Transfer heat between cells
- Worker 4: Process chemical reactions (planned)

These workers communicate through shared memory — a special block of memory that all threads can read and write simultaneously, coordinated through atomic operations (a way to ensure only one thread writes to a cell at a time).

### How Creatures Are Stored and Simulated

Creatures are not stored as traditional objects. Instead, the game uses an Entity Component System (ECS) — a data structure optimized for processing thousands of game objects simultaneously.

In an ECS:
- Every creature has an ID number (entity)
- Data is stored in flat arrays organized by type (components): all X positions in one array, all Y positions in another, all health values in another, etc.
- Systems process entire arrays at once — far faster than accessing individual objects

This means the game can efficiently process 10,000 creatures simultaneously. The renderer draws all visible creatures in a single draw call (one instruction to the graphics card) using instanced rendering — like stamping the same creature shape 10,000 times with different positions and colors.

### How Time Works

The simulation clock is the most distinctive feature of the engine. Real time and simulation time are separate. The player can set a "time scale" — how many simulation seconds pass per real second.

At 1x: 1 real second = 1 simulation second
At 1,000,000x: 1 real second = 11.5 days of simulation time

This allows the simulation to span cosmological timescales. The epoch system maps simulation time to real cosmological eras — from the Planck epoch (10^-43 seconds after the Big Bang) to the Dark Era (after all stars have burned out, 10^40 years in the future).

### How Chemistry Works

Every chemical reaction has a rate that depends on temperature (via the Arrhenius equation). At room temperature, iron rusts slowly. Increase the temperature to 800°C and iron oxidizes rapidly. The simulation checks every cell, looks at what chemicals are present and at what temperature, and applies reaction rates accordingly.

Reactions consume reactants and produce products. Combustion consumes fuel and oxygen, produces carbon dioxide and water, and releases heat. Photosynthesis consumes carbon dioxide and water, uses light energy, and produces glucose. Each reaction has real energy values.

### How Genetics Works

Each creature's traits are encoded in 256 bits — equivalent to a 32-character text string. These bits encode everything about the creature: how big it is, how fast it moves, how smart it is, how it reproduces.

When two creatures reproduce sexually, their genomes are mixed through chromosomal crossover (the same process that occurs in real sexual reproduction): the offspring's genome is assembled from alternating segments of the two parents' genomes. Mutations then introduce small random changes.

The phenotype (the actual traits the creature expresses) is decoded from the genotype (the raw bit sequence) using dominant/recessive logic: some bit patterns override others, just like how brown eyes are dominant over blue eyes in humans.

### How AI Works

Creatures at different intelligence levels work completely differently:

A Level 0 microbe simply detects chemicals and moves toward food. No planning, no memory.

A Level 3 creature (great ape intelligence) receives information about the world through 32 vision rays, chemical sensors, and body state monitors. It has a set of goals (survive, reproduce, build shelter) and uses A* search to find the shortest sequence of actions that achieves those goals. If it is hungry and a fruit tree is nearby, the plan might be: move to tree → pick fruit → eat fruit. If it is starving and being chased, it might abandon eating and flee first.

A Level 4 creature (human intelligence) does all of the above, but can also generate language and complex reasoning by querying an actual AI language model. An NPC might say "I distrust you because you took from our food stores last season" — a response generated by Claude or GPT given full context about the NPC's history, emotions, and knowledge.

---

## 9. Performance Analysis

### Architecture-Level Observations

The project has made several excellent performance decisions:

**SharedArrayBuffer and Web Workers** — The most computationally intensive work (physics, fluid, thermal) runs on background threads, keeping the main thread free for rendering. This is the right architectural choice for a simulation of this scale.

**Instanced Rendering** — All 10,000 potential creatures are drawn in a single GPU draw call. This is the correct approach for large numbers of similar objects.

**Typed Arrays in ECS** — bitecs stores all component data in typed arrays (Float32Array, Uint32Array, etc.), which are cache-friendly and can be processed very quickly by modern CPUs.

**Genome SharedArrayBuffer** — The 100,000-creature genome buffer (100,000 × 32 bytes = 3.2 MB) is allocated in shared memory, allowing workers to access genetic data without copying.

### Potential Bottlenecks

**Chemistry on Main Thread** — The ReactionEngine currently runs on the main thread (the chemistry worker is a placeholder). Processing 131,072 cells with 60+ reactions per tick on the main thread will cause frame rate drops when fully implemented. This will need to move to the chemistry worker.

**Grid Size** — 131,072 cells at 25 Float32 values each = 13.1 MB of SharedArrayBuffer. This is manageable, but if the world needs to be larger, chunk streaming (WorldChunkManager, not yet implemented) will be required.

**LLM Latency** — Level 4 AI queries an external AI API. Each query takes hundreds of milliseconds to seconds. A queue-based system is implemented (to avoid blocking), but with many high-intelligence NPCs, the queue could become a bottleneck.

**No Level of Detail (LOD)** — The creature renderer uses the same sphere geometry for all creatures regardless of distance. For 10,000 creatures, LOD (using simpler geometry for distant creatures) would improve performance.

---

## 10. Accessibility Review

### Current State

The current interface has minimal accessibility features. What exists:

**Positive:**
- Time control buttons have `aria-label` attributes ("Resume", "Pause", "Time scale 1×")
- Time scale buttons have `aria-pressed` state (indicating which speed is active)
- Pause/resume buttons have `title` attributes for tooltip text

**Missing:**
- No keyboard navigation for player movement (no player controller exists yet)
- No color-blind modes — the vital bars rely entirely on color (red = health, orange = hunger, etc.)
- Text sizes are very small (9-11px for most labels) — below comfortable reading size
- No screen reader support for the 3D scene
- No high-contrast mode
- No font size scaling
- The glassmorphism style (semi-transparent panels) can reduce text readability

### Assessment

The project is at an early stage where accessibility has not yet been a focus. As gameplay features are added, accessibility considerations should be incorporated from the start — particularly keyboard controls for all actions, color-blind alternatives for vital bars, and appropriate ARIA roles for game panels.

---

## 11. Security Assessment

### Current Security Posture

The application is a client-side browser game. There is no user authentication, no server, no database of user information. The primary security concern is the LLM Bridge.

**LLM API Key Exposure**

The LLMBridge (`src/ai/LLMBridge.ts`) connects to Anthropic, OpenAI, or Ollama. If API keys are stored in the frontend code (rather than proxied through a server), they would be exposed to anyone who views the browser's network traffic.

The design document mentions a Rust server (`/rust/`) and a Node server (`/server/`), both of which are empty stubs. The intended architecture presumably has API keys stored on the server, with the client making requests to the server, which proxies to the LLM providers. This architecture has not yet been implemented.

**SharedArrayBuffer Security Requirements**

The `vite.config.ts` correctly sets the required headers for SharedArrayBuffer:
- `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin`
- `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`

These headers are required by browsers to allow SharedArrayBuffer (which was disabled following the Spectre vulnerability). The headers are correctly configured in the development server. They will also need to be configured on the production hosting service.

**WebAssembly (WASM)**

Rapier3D runs as WebAssembly compiled from Rust. WASM is executed in a sandboxed environment — it cannot access the file system or network directly. This is standard and safe.

**No User Data Collection**

The game does not collect, transmit, or store any personal data. No cookies, no analytics, no user accounts. This is appropriate for the current stage.

---

## 12. Code Quality

### Overall Assessment

The code quality is high for a project at this stage. The codebase demonstrates:

**Strengths:**

- **Scientific accuracy** — Every constant, every equation, every algorithm is sourced from real science. Comments frequently cite the source (e.g., "Based on Jeff Orkin's F.E.A.R. implementation", "NIST CODATA 2022", "Whittaker biome classification").

- **TypeScript strict mode** — The `tsconfig.json` uses strict mode, which catches many categories of bugs at compile time. All types are explicit and well-defined.

- **Clear interfaces** — Every major data structure is defined as a TypeScript interface. `SensoryInput`, `MotorOutput`, `Phenotype`, `NPCContext`, `PlanetConfig` — all are clearly typed.

- **Separation of concerns** — Physics, chemistry, biology, AI, world generation, and rendering are each in their own directory with minimal coupling. The ECS architecture enforces this separation.

- **Meaningful constants** — Physical constants are named and grouped (`PHYSICS.G`, `THERMO.Cp_water`), not scattered as magic numbers.

- **Good inline comments** — Complex algorithms include plain-language explanations of what they are doing and why.

**Weaknesses:**

- **Missing tests** — `package.json` includes vitest as a dependency, but no test files exist anywhere in the project. Given the complexity of the simulation math, tests would be highly valuable (verifying that the Arrhenius equation returns the correct rate for methane combustion at 500°C, for example).

- **Missing connections** — Many systems are implemented in isolation but not wired to each other or to the main game loop. A chemistry engine exists, a biology engine exists, but they do not yet interact.

- **Placeholder workers** — The chemistry worker (`chem.worker.ts`) receives messages but does nothing with tick messages. This is a stub that could confuse a new developer.

- **No error handling in workers** — If a worker thread crashes, there is no recovery mechanism.

- **No save system** — The IndexedDB library (idb) is a dependency, but no save system code exists. All game state is lost on page refresh.

---

---

## 14. APIs and Integrations

### LLM Integration

The LLMBridge supports three providers:

**Anthropic Claude**
- Endpoint: `https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages`
- Model: configurable (default would likely be a claude-3 or claude-sonnet variant)
- Auth: Bearer token (API key)

**OpenAI GPT**
- Endpoint: `https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions`
- Model: configurable
- Auth: Bearer token (API key)

**Ollama (local)**
- Endpoint: `http://localhost:11434/api/chat`
- Auth: none (local service)
- Allows fully offline operation for LLM features

All three use the same interface — switching providers requires only a configuration change. Requests are queued, have a default 10-second timeout, and support cancellation via AbortController.

### No Other External APIs

The simulation is entirely self-contained. No telemetry, no analytics, no CDN dependencies at runtime. All assets are bundled.

---

## 15. Data and Database

### Current Data Storage

No persistent data storage is currently implemented. All game state exists only in JavaScript memory and is lost when the page is closed or refreshed.

### Planned Data Storage (Not Yet Implemented)

The `idb` library (IndexedDB wrapper) is installed, suggesting plans for a browser-based save system using IndexedDB — the browser's built-in key-value database for structured data. IndexedDB can store:

- Player state (vitals, evolution points, discoveries)
- World state (the 3D grid, creature positions, species registries)
- Technology research progress
- Civilization state

Given the grid size (13.1 MB for the physics grid alone, plus creature data), save file sizes could become substantial. The design mentions a Rust server (`/rust/`), suggesting server-side saves may also be planned.

### In-Memory Data

| Data | Size | Storage |
|------|------|---------|
| Physics grid | ~13.1 MB | SharedArrayBuffer |
| Genome buffer (100k creatures) | 3.2 MB | SharedArrayBuffer |
| ECS component arrays | Variable | Typed arrays |
| Zustand stores | Kilobytes | JavaScript memory |
| Element database (118 elements) | ~50 KB | JavaScript object |
| Tech tree (150 nodes) | ~100 KB | JavaScript array |

---

## 16. Deployment and Infrastructure

### Current Development Setup

The application runs entirely in the browser. Development requires only:

```
npm install
npm run dev
```

The Vite dev server runs on localhost. The COOP/COEP headers required for SharedArrayBuffer are configured in `vite.config.ts`.

### Build Configuration

- **Target**: `esnext` — modern JavaScript, no transpilation for older browsers
- **Workers**: ES module format (not the older legacy format)
- **WASM**: Supported via `vite-plugin-wasm` and `vite-plugin-top-level-await`
- **Rapier3D**: Excluded from Vite's pre-bundling optimization (it handles its own WASM loading)

### Production Deployment Considerations

For production deployment:

1. **COOP/COEP Headers are required** — The hosting service must set `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` and `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`. Not all hosting services support custom headers. Vercel, Netlify, and Cloudflare Pages all support this configuration.

2. **LLM API keys must not be in the frontend** — If LLM features are enabled, a server-side proxy is required. The placeholder `/server/` and `/rust/` directories suggest this is planned.

3. **No CI/CD pipeline exists** — No GitHub Actions or other automation is configured. Deployment would be manual.

4. **No Docker configuration** — No containerization is present.

---

## 17. Known Issues and Gaps

### Critical — Would Break Functionality

| Issue | Impact |
|-------|--------|
| No player controller | The game cannot be played. The player cannot move, interact, or control anything. |
| Chemistry worker is a placeholder | Chemical reactions are not simulated in the worker threads. The ReactionEngine runs on the main thread (planned) but is not yet called from anywhere. |
| No creature spawning | The creature renderer exists, but no code spawns creatures into the world. The 3D scene is empty. |
| No game loop connecting systems | Physics, chemistry, biology, AI, and player systems are each implemented but none are connected to each other or to a running game loop. |
| No save system | All progress is lost on page refresh. |

### High — Major Feature Missing

| Issue | Impact |
|-------|--------|
| No inventory or crafting | The survival and civilization systems cannot function without item management. |
| No building system | Civilization progression requires construction. |
| No abiogenesis event | The game's first milestone (life emerging from chemistry) has no implementation. |
| No main menu or tutorial | New players have no guidance. |
| Metabolism system not connected | Player vitals do not change in real time. |
| No discovery journal | Players cannot track what they have learned. |

### Medium — Incomplete Implementation

| Issue | Impact |
|-------|--------|
| LLM API keys in frontend | Security concern for production. |
| No error recovery for workers | A crashed worker ends the simulation silently. |
| No test coverage | Any change to physics constants or equations could break the simulation with no automated detection. |
| No LOD for creature renderer | Performance will degrade with many creatures visible. |
| Small text sizes in HUD | Text at 9-11px is hard to read, especially on high-DPI screens. |

### Low — Polish and Enhancement

| Issue | Impact |
|-------|--------|
| No color-blind mode | Vital bars use only color to convey status. |
| Genome buffer for 100,000 creatures allocated at startup | Memory is allocated even when few creatures exist. |
| No loading indicator for WASM | Rapier3D WASM loading is asynchronous; no progress shown to user. |

---

## 18. Notable Features and Highlights

These are the aspects of the project that deserve particular recognition:

### 1. Scientific Authenticity at Every Level

This project does not fake science. It uses real equations:
- Arrhenius kinetics for chemical reactions
- Fourier's law for heat conduction
- Stefan-Boltzmann law for thermal radiation
- Generalized Lotka-Volterra for ecosystems
- NIST CODATA 2022 physical constants (exact values from the international standards body)
- ISA lapse rate for temperature vs. altitude
- Hadley cell circulation for precipitation patterns
- Kleiber's law for metabolic rate scaling

This level of scientific rigor in a game is extraordinarily unusual.

### 2. Cosmological Time Scale

The epoch system is a beautiful design feature. The game simulates cosmological time — from the Planck epoch (10^-43 seconds after the Big Bang) to the Dark Era (10^40 years in the future). The time display formats simulation time across 83 orders of magnitude, from nanoseconds to gigayears. The epoch names are drawn from real cosmological physics.

### 3. Five-Tier Neural Architecture

The creature intelligence system elegantly spans from purely reactive microbes to human-equivalent planners to LLM-backed reasoners. Each tier is a genuinely different type of AI algorithm — stimulus-response, FSM, Q-learning, GOAP, and LLM integration — not just a quantitative upgrade.

### 4. Full 118-Element Database

All 118 elements of the periodic table are modeled with real physical properties. This is not a game that abstracts away material science — it models which elements are conductors, which are radioactive, which are biologically necessary.

### 5. Six-Type Mutation Model

The mutation engine models six distinct types of genetic mutation (point, insertion, deletion, transposition, inversion, duplication) plus horizontal gene transfer — more variety than most biology textbooks describe for a game engine.

### 6. OCC Emotion Model

Using the Ortony-Clore-Collins cognitive model for creature emotions gives the AI a coherent emotional psychology rooted in actual emotion theory, rather than simple happy/sad sliders.

### 7. Red Queen Arms Race Tracking

The ecosystem dynamically tracks predator-prey arms races and detects when evolutionary escalation is occurring — a sophisticated ecological concept rarely modeled in games.

### 8. Technology Tree with Historical Analogs

Each of the 150 technology nodes includes a historical analog — the real-world technology or discovery it represents. This is both educational and grounds the game's progression in actual human history.

### 9. LLM NPC Dialogue

The integration of actual AI language models for NPC dialogue — with full context including emotions, memories, relationships, and history with the player — would produce genuinely emergent conversations if connected to the game.

### 10. Physical Constants Control as End Game

The ultimate game goal — gaining the ability to modify the laws of physics — is philosophically profound. It raises questions about the nature of simulated realities and the relationship between creator and creation that few games have explored.

---

## 19. Recommendations

### Priority 1: Create a Playable Prototype (Immediate)

The single most important next step is making the game playable in any form. This does not require all systems to be connected — it requires:

1. Implement `PlayerController.ts` — basic WASD movement in the 3D world
2. Connect the metabolism system to the running game loop so vitals change over time
3. Spawn at least one creature (the player entity) into the world
4. Add a basic food item the player can pick up and eat
5. Connect health to death (game over when health reaches 0)

This gives the player something to do and allows testing of the core game feel.

### Priority 2: Connect the Chemistry and Biology Systems

The chemistry and biology engines are both implemented but disconnected. The next step after a playable prototype is:

1. Move ReactionEngine to the chemistry worker
2. Spawn some chemical compounds in the world grid
3. Create a simple abiogenesis event that spawns the first organism from chemistry
4. Connect the EcosystemBalance system to actual creature populations

### Priority 3: Implement Inventory and Crafting

Without inventory, the survival and civilization phases cannot function. The `Inventory.ts` file needs to be created and connected to the player entity.

### Priority 4: Add Test Coverage

The simulation math is complex. Any change to a constant or equation could break the simulation in subtle ways. Tests for:
- Arrhenius rate calculations at specific temperatures
- Genome encoding/decoding round-trips
- Lotka-Volterra integration accuracy
- Epoch boundary calculations

...would prevent regressions and give confidence when making changes.

### Priority 5: Secure the LLM Integration

Before deploying to production:
- Implement the planned server proxy (`/server/`) to hold API keys
- Never expose API keys in frontend code
- Add rate limiting to prevent API cost overruns

### Priority 6: Accessibility from the Start

As gameplay features are added:
- Use relative font sizes (rem) instead of fixed pixel sizes
- Add keyboard navigation for all UI interactions
- Add ARIA live regions for vital bar changes
- Consider a colorblind mode (shape or text labels in addition to color)

### Quick Wins

| Action | Effort | Impact |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Spawn one creature (the player) at world start | Low | Makes the world feel alive |
| Connect metabolism system to game loop | Low | Vitals start changing |
| Add a basic food pickup | Low | First gameplay moment |
| Add error handling to workers | Low | Prevents silent failures |
| Increase HUD text size to 12-13px minimum | Very low | Immediate readability improvement |

### Long-Term Vision

The game design is extraordinarily ambitious — potentially years of development away from the full vision. The recommendation is to ship incremental milestones as standalone experiences:
- "Phase 1: Chemistry" — A standalone chemistry sandbox
- "Phase 2: Evolution" — A standalone evolution simulator
- "Phase 3: Civilization" — The full connected experience

This allows the project to gain players and feedback at each stage rather than waiting for the complete experience.

---

## 20. Summary Scorecard

| Area | Rating | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Vision and Concept | Excellent | One of the most ambitious game concepts ever attempted in a browser |
| Scientific Accuracy | Excellent | Real equations, real constants, real algorithms throughout |
| Code Architecture | Excellent | ECS, workers, shared memory — correctly designed for scale |
| Code Quality | Good | Clean, well-typed, well-commented; lacks tests |
| Current Playability | Critical | The game cannot currently be played in any meaningful way |
| Feature Completeness | Needs Improvement | Core systems written but not connected; many planned files missing |
| User Interface | Needs Improvement | HUD works but there is no gameplay interface yet |
| Performance Design | Good | Correct architectural choices; some bottlenecks to address |
| Accessibility | Needs Improvement | Minimal accessibility; needs attention as features are added |
| Security | Needs Improvement | LLM key exposure risk for production; fine for development |
| Testing | Critical | No test files exist for a heavily mathematical codebase |
| Documentation | Excellent | Comprehensive 1,067-line design document; good inline comments |
| Deployment Readiness | Needs Improvement | No CI/CD, no production server, no save system |
| **Overall Project Health** | **Early Development** | **Exceptional foundation; substantial work remains before playability** |
